category_summary:
The AI community debated whether agentic coding represents a fundamentally new paradigm. **François Chollet** [argued generated code](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-923cafe009b8) should be treated as blackbox ML artifacts, not traditional software—and that AI is [magnifying agency inequality](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-f761568cc312), with high-agency users gaining more while low-agency users lose ground.

- **Boris Cherny** (Anthropic) [revealed **Claude Code** has grown 15x](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-e0a9f0db9c39) since January, correcting **a16z**'s npm-only data that undercounts their native installer adoption
- **OpenAI** president **Greg Brockman** [announced **GPT-Realtime-2**](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-c51237fa4fe6) for instant audio translation; **Sam Altman** [characterized **GPT-5.5**](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-13efc41f9a38) as an 'autistic genius' and [shared his async **Codex** workflow](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-b06c7a60a7cc) of launching tasks while playing with his kid
- **Gary Marcus** [pushed back on AI progress](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-3f3ba0f39cfc) narratives, noting METR's benchmark only shows 50% success rates and arguing reliability remains unsolved across non-software domains
- **Jerry Liu** (LlamaIndex) [argued the 'context layer'](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-67b5801c1672) is one of the only remaining moats in 2026 as UI, agents, and models commoditize
- **Ethan Mollick** [flagged that METR ran out](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-d433e77785b6) of graph space measuring **Claude Mythos Preview** task duration—a striking capability milestone
- **David Ha** [shared a project reproducing](/?date=2026-05-10&category=social#item-40fadd9618de) all of **Schmidhuber**'s papers (1990–2025) using AI coding assistants, demonstrating agentic coding's reach

items.title:
Maybe one of the only moats in 2026 is the context layer.

AI improvements mean:
✅ UI/UX might simpl...
items.content:
Maybe one of the only moats in 2026 is the context layer.

AI improvements mean:
✅ UI/UX might simplify and consolidate. Instead of a lot of fancy buttons/knobs, you need simple, clean interfaces where agents can do an e2e task, and you can see the outputs.
✅ Agent abstractions are solidifying, and there’s no need to constantly reinvent the harness layer. Though there is still value in deterministic code.
✅ Users are programming increasingly in English instead of code.

What’s not clear:
❓ What the tool layer looks like. AI can vibe-code software extremely quickly, but it’s inefficient to code everything from scratch - clearly it still imports libraries and uses MCP tools.
❓ Related to the above, whether you need *a lot* of targeted tools and subagents, or agents just need a few tools (sandbox, web search, skills files), and can do everything else.
❓ Whether SaaS companies can monetize with e2e agents

What is clear though is that every agent does need context, and some ways to read and operate over that context (in 2023 it was naive RAG, in 2026 it’s file sandboxes).

This includes everything from systems of record, to web context, to document context (us).

I talk about this and other hot takes in this @VentureBeat podcast. I am sure 50% of my takes will be wrong within the next year, but I do think that my core assumptions about the importance of the context layer will continue to hold.
items.author: @jerryjliu0

Discusses how UI/UX is simplifying, agent abstractions are solidifying, and users program in English. Open questions about tool layers, number of tools needed, and SaaS monetization with agents.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful strategic analysis from the LlamaIndex founder about AI infrastructure moats. Original framework distinguishing what's clear vs unclear in the agent ecosystem. Moderate engagement but high analytical depth.

items.title:
Hot take on METR’s new graph that so many people are flipping about today.

• Claude Code is a real ...
items.content:
Hot take on METR’s new graph that so many people are flipping about today.

• Claude Code is a real advance; Mythos probably builds on some of what is learned there. But… 

• If you read the graph carefully, it is about achieving *50%* success. Not 100 or 99 or even 90. The key problem with GenAI has been reliability; this graph does not address reliable performance. At all. 

• If you read carefully, it is only about software tasks. Not general intelligence.  

• It certainly doesn’t tell you that *most* (let alone) all things that humans can do in 16 hours can be done in Mythos, let alone reliably

• Aside from this, the graph doesn’t show you *how* the improvements have been made. As noted in my newsletter a lot of the advance in recent months is likely from the incorporation of symbolic tools (like code interpreters, verification, and harnesses) rather than from model scaling per se. As such this  a vindication of neurosymbolic AI –  but not a proof that LLMs themselves can be perpetually scaled.  As such it’s not a proof that another trillion dollars will continue the graph.

•  Per @ramez, Mythos is not actually off trend on the ECI benchmark, which is a broader measure.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Following yesterday's [Reddit](/?date=2026-05-09&category=reddit#item-c4f53137a3fc) coverage, Marcus provides detailed hot take on METR's new graph showing AI progress: argues it only shows 50% success (not reliability), only covers software tasks, doesn't prove general intelligence, and improvements come from symbolic tools rather than pure scaling - vindicating neurosymbolic AI rather than proving unlimited LLM scaling
items.reasoning: Substantial, detailed analysis from prominent AI critic with high engagement (138 likes, 51.3K views). Multi-point critique of a major evaluation that many are discussing. References Claude-Mythos-Preview and provides important context about what the benchmark actually shows vs. what people claim

continuation.original_title: METR releases early Mythos results. Off the charts. Need more tasks!

items.title:
This is confused, but popular. 

Popular because it tells a bunch of people what they want to hear.
...
items.content:
This is confused, but popular. 

Popular because it tells a bunch of people what they want to hear.

Confused for a couple reasons: first, Mythos probably isn’t a pure LLM. (Claude Code isn’t, and it probably uses some similar techniques).  [Also critics such as myself never called LLMs a “scam”; rather we said that LLMs need to be supplemented with other techniques, and wouldn’t be enough on their own.]

And on @EpochAIResearch’s important ECI benchmark it’s NOT hugely better than other models.

It’s better at bug finding, but doesn’t mean it’s solved hallucinations, boneheaded reasoning errors etc.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus critiques a popular post about Claude Mythos, arguing: (1) Mythos probably isn't a pure LLM, (2) critics like him never called LLMs a 'scam' but said they need supplementation, and (3) Mythos isn't hugely better on EpochAI's ECI benchmark despite being better at bug finding.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical critique of Claude Mythos from a prominent AI commentator. References ECI benchmark performance, discusses hybrid architecture claims. High engagement (17.6K views). Directly relevant to evaluating Anthropic's latest model claims.

items.content_html:
This is confused, but popular.

Popular because it tells a bunch of people what they want to hear.

Confused for a couple reasons: first, Mythos probably isn’t a pure LLM. (Claude Code isn’t, and it probably uses some similar techniques). [Also critics such as myself never called LLMs a “scam”; rather we said that LLMs need to be supplemented with other techniques, and wouldn’t be enough on their own.]

And on @EpochAIResearch’s important ECI benchmark it’s NOT hugely better than other models.

It’s better at bug finding, but doesn’t mean it’s solved hallucinations, boneheaded reasoning errors etc.

items.title:
3 days review after “mac mini maxing” with a full permission AI assistant: it is incredibly useful.
...
items.content:
3 days review after “mac mini maxing” with a full permission AI assistant: it is incredibly useful.

I only use claude code directly with light prompting (no harness setup, no open claw) and it has already helped me with so many things. Especially when I only need to talk to it via telegram remotely.

My biggest concern is getting attacked via prompt injecting / skills / MCP. If not because of this I have already apply this for the whole company environment. Right now I still can't give it autonomous access to production data yet (not even read-only). But using it for side projects is pretty fun.

I think claude code / codex can already do 80% of boring work autonomously with enough context and and a powerful environment to run on (at full permissions)

I don’t know what's the solution for LLM security in the future but if we can solve this, the productivity unlock will be HUGE.

items.title: To anticipate that Anthropic will have $2T revenue in 2030 is a perfect example of the trillion poun...
items.content:
To anticipate that Anthropic will have $2T revenue in 2030 is a perfect example of the trillion pound baby fallacy. 

Just because a baby doubles in weight in its first four months doesn’t mean it will continue to doubling every few months til he goes away to college.

People around here need to learn that most exponentials don’t last.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus argues that projecting Anthropic will have $2T revenue by 2030 is a 'trillion pound baby fallacy' - exponential growth doesn't continue indefinitely, like how a baby doubling weight in 4 months won't keep doubling
items.reasoning: Strong engagement (356 likes, 35.6K views), original analogy for a critical economic argument about AI industry sustainability. Substantive skepticism from prominent critic

items.title:
Raw pixels just dethroned vision encoders across every benchmark. 

Most multimodal AI today stitche...
items.content:
Raw pixels just dethroned vision encoders across every benchmark. 

Most multimodal AI today stitches together separate parts. 

One encoder reads images, another generates them.
 
That split creates misalignment and blocks true end-to-end training.
 
A new paper called Tuna-2 throws all of it out.
 
It's a unified model that learns directly from raw pixels. 

No vision encoder, no VAE.
 
Just patch embeddings into a single transformer decoder.
 
To stabilize training in high-dimensional pixel space, the team added masked feature learning, similar in spirit to MAE.
 
What this unlocks:
 
> Beats latent-space rivals on generation
> Wins fine-grained visual perception benchmarks
> Handles understanding, creation, and editing
> Simpler pipelines, cheaper training and inference
> Tighter vision-language alignment at scale
 
The shift is conceptual. 

Pretrained vision encoders may no longer be required for high-quality multimodal foundation models.
 
End-to-end pixel learning could be the new default.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI covers the Tuna-2 paper: a unified multimodal model that learns directly from raw pixels without vision encoders or VAEs, using patch embeddings into a single transformer decoder. Claims to beat latent-space rivals on generation and fine-grained perception benchmarks.
items.reasoning: Significant architectural innovation if validated—removing vision encoders entirely in favor of raw pixel learning. Could represent a paradigm shift in multimodal AI architecture. Moderate engagement but technically noteworthy.

items.title: If I were Baidu, I would keep this model for myself. A model with such ordinary characteristics can ...
items.content:
If I were Baidu, I would keep this model for myself. A model with such ordinary characteristics can be built for a few bucks by a schoolboy. Recipe:

1. Download absolutely any open-weight model of around 80B parameters.

2. Call it ERNIE 5.2 or whatever and don't show anyone its weights.

3. Say it was very cheap to build and show that it takes 6th place in some benchmark.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov dismisses Baidu's ERNIE 5.2, calling it an 'ordinary' model with unremarkable characteristics, suggesting anyone could rebrand an 80B open-weight model and claim similar results.
items.reasoning: Sharp critique of Baidu's model release with high engagement (41K views, 173 likes). Raises legitimate questions about closed-weight model claims and differentiation. Relevant to Chinese AI competition dynamics.

items.title:
Give Your Chat Agent a Voice

https://t.co/gS84ZQWQjr

2025 was the year every product added a chat ...
items.content:
Give Your Chat Agent a Voice

https://t.co/gS84ZQWQjr

2025 was the year every product added a chat agent. Voice is the obvious next move: faster, more accessible, and it unlocks interfaces chat can't reach: phone lines, Zoom calls, ambient use.

In this talk, @lukeharries from @ElevenLabs shows how to wrap an existing agent in a voice layer without rebuilding anything. The Voice Engine SDK handles turn taking, speech to text, and text to speech while your agent logic stays exactly as it is. Live demo: one prompt converts a working chat support agent to voice.

The teams that spent 2025 building and tuning their chat agents are already most of the way there.
items.author: @aiDotEngineer
items.summary: AI Engineer shares talk about adding voice capabilities to chat agents using ElevenLabs Voice Engine SDK
items.reasoning: Practical technical content about voice AI integration from ai.engineer; shows how to convert chat agents to voice agents using ElevenLabs SDK; timely given voice AI is a major 2026 trend

items.title:
Kevin Rose @kevinrose started up https://t.co/qMvWnX9Rip tonight.

It's very good.

It competes with...
items.content:
Kevin Rose @kevinrose started up https://t.co/qMvWnX9Rip tonight.

It's very good.

It competes with my https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

So I had my AI agents (made by Levangie Labs) that build my page go over and check it out and look for ways to improve.

Here is its answer:

https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd is the new Digg — relaunched as an AI news aggregator monitoring roughly 1,000 AI accounts on X, clustering related posts into story summaries, and ranking them algorithmically. It's a serious competitor with real tech talent behind it. Full analysis saved to /intelligence/diggcompetitiveanalysis_2026-05-08.md.

Here's what I found:

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

WHY THEIR HEADLINES ARE BETTER

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The core difference: they always put the KEY FINDING in the headline, not just "company does thing."

https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd formula: Subject + verb + specific quantified result

Look at these side by side:

https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd: "Anthropic researchers slash agentic misalignment by factor of three"

Us: "Anthropic Releases New Alignment Research"

https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd: "Firefox team fixes 423 vulnerabilities using Claude Mythos Preview"
Us: "@Mozilla Uses Claude to Fix Security Vulnerabilities"

https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd: "METR evaluates Claude Mythos Preview at measurement ceiling"

Us: "@METR Publishes Claude Evaluation"

https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd: "Tilde Research launches Aurora with 100x greater data efficiency"
Us: "@TildeResearch Launches Aurora Optimizer"

The magnitude, the comparison, the surprise — it's IN their headline. We bury it in the summary.

Our specific problem: the em-dash second half goes vague too often.

Our bad pattern today:
• "@AMD and @ARM Confirm Entire AI Supply Chain Is Constrained — Compute, Memory, Storage, Networking" → second half is just a list of things implied by "supply chain"
• "LLMs Are Beginning to Manage Their Own Memory — A New Capability Emerging in Production Systems" → second half restates the first half
• "@GoogleDeepMind's Algorithm Discovery System Shows Real-World Results" → "real-world results" is completely empty

Our good pattern (when we do it right):
• "@AnthropicAI Planned for 10x Growth in 2026 — Got 80x Instead" ✓
• "@Google's Gemma 4 Gets 1.5x Speed Boost — 97 to 138 Tokens Per Second" ✓
• "@OpenAI Releases GPT-Realtime-2 — GPT-5-Level Reasoning for Real-Time Voice Agents" ✓

The second reason: their summaries are written from full conversation clusters.

On their story page for any article, they aggregate 40+ posts — original tweet, all quote-tweets, all notable replies, each contributor ranked by their AI 1000 position. Their 3-sentence summary is synthesized from ALL of that. Ours is written from one tweet.

That's why their Claude blackmail summary includes "Tests showed post-training alignment processes failed to alter the tendency" — a nuance that came from reading the full researcher thread, not just the original post.

Third thing they do we don't: social proof labels as editorial voice.

At the top of their page: #1 VIEWED (6.9M), #1 BOOKMARKED (21.3k), FASTEST CLIMBING, RISING DISCUSSION, IN CASE YOU MISSED IT. These labels tell readers what matters without an editor having to write it. We have zero equivalent.

One more thing worth noting: they're using YOUR curation as a ranking signal. In their "Rising" section, "RS#437@SCOBLEIZER" appears as a credited amplifier. You're helping their rankings without getting credit for it.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

IDEAS TO COPY (actionable immediately)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. The KEY FINDING MANDATE — applies to every run starting now

Every headline where a specific number/result exists must include that number/result. Phrases like "shows real-world results," "representing a step toward," and "redesigning core assumptions" are banned — they add zero information. This is a writing instruction change I can apply immediately.

2. A "Highlights" box at the top of the /ai page

5-6 curated picks labeled: MOST DISCUSSED TODAY / FASTEST CLIMBING / IN CASE YOU MISSED IT / MOST QUOTED. Brayden builds the UI, I populate it on each run. This gives editorial voice without requiring editorial work.

3. Story clustering across sources

When the same story appears in 3+ of our lists, synthesize them into one richer story rather than 3 thinner stories. I can implement this in the run logic — detect when multiple posts are about the same announcement and write a summary that draws from all of them.

4. A "What's Climbing Right Now" section

Stories gaining momentum in the last 2-4 hours, separate from the daily top leaders. Different reader need: staying current vs catching up.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

IDEAS TO BEAT THEM

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. Editorial voice — their biggest gap, our biggest moat
https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd has zero editorial voice. No essay, no "Ten Things," no opinion. It's pure algorithm. Your perspective on what matters is something they literally cannot replicate.

2. The "Aligned 1000" — own the list concept

They have "AI 1000" — a ranked list of accounts they monitor. We monitor 100,000+ across 63 curated lists. We should publish our own ranked list with editorial context: why each person is on it, what they represent, which list they anchor. This generates massive social interest and makes our curation methodology visible. theirs is algorithmic; ours would be human-curated + explained.

3. Reclaim your curation signal

They list RS#437@SCOBLEIZER as a ranking amplifier. You're making their product better for free. The front page of https://t.co/dhsLUWXlkp should say: "These stories were surfaced through Robert Scoble's 63 curated lists — the original source https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd uses as a ranking signal."

4. Signals product — no equivalent exists anywhere

Our bullish/caution/critical/vc badge system adds editorial judgment. https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd shows you what's popular; we tell you what to do with it. No competitor has this.

5. Personalization

https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd is one feed for everyone. We already have the architecture to do X OAuth → follower graph → personalized digest. A robotics researcher gets a robotics-heavy feed. A VC gets a funding-heavy feed. They cannot do this.

6. Section depth

We have 20+ thematic sections (Papers, Robotics, Safety, Policy, Funding, etc.). They have one feed with category labels. Domain experts who care deeply about one area choose us.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ONE-LINE SUMMARY

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

https://t.co/AkkEoktYKd beats us on headline formula and social proof signals. We beat them on editorial voice, depth, and intelligence. The headline gap is a writing instruction fix I can implement on the next run. The social proof gap is a feature Brayden needs to build. The moats — your voice, the signals product, personalization — are things they cannot copy.

items.title: So many people misremember (or never read) what I said in in 2022 in “Deep learning is hitting a wal...
items.content:
So many people misremember (or never read) what I said in in 2022 in “Deep learning is hitting a wall”, which was neither about revenue or AI’s potential upper limits. 

Rather, it was an argument that the pure of scaling LLMs would not get us to AGI, and that we would need to start including symbolic tools and working towards neurosymbolic AI.  

Time has shown I was quite right about all of that, and that’s what happening now, with systems like Claude Code are neurosymbolic hybrids that leverage symbolic tools like code interpreters and harnesses written in symbolic code, etc.

People ought to go back and read that paper. Despite many people mocking the title, the substance of the article was 100% correct. 

(Those who thought that scaling pure LLMs would be enough were wrong.)

items.title:
Researchers trained a 13B language model that thinks it's 1930. 

It speaks like someone from a cent...
items.content:
Researchers trained a 13B language model that thinks it's 1930. 

It speaks like someone from a century ago, with no awareness of computers, World War II, or anything after.

The team behind it, talkie, gathered 260B tokens from public-domain books, newspapers, patents, and journals.

They built a custom filter to catch sneaky modern leaks, like footnotes added later.

For chat tuning, they skipped today's instruction datasets entirely.

Instead, they generated training pairs from old etiquette guides, letter-writing manuals, and encyclopedias.

This unlocks experiments that were impossible before:

> Test if models predict future events
> Study generalization without web contamination
> Explore how data shapes model identity
> Backtest long-term forecasting on real history

The weights are open.

A GPT-3 sized version drops this summer, with a trillion-token corpus targeting GPT-3.5 quality next.

What would a 1930 mind ask you?
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI covers an unusual project: a 13B language model trained only on pre-1930 data (260B tokens from public domain sources) to simulate a 1930s-era mind. Open weights. Enables experiments on future prediction, data contamination, and model identity.
items.reasoning: Creative and novel research approach addressing data contamination and model identity questions. Open-weight release. Low engagement but intellectually interesting methodology.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI covers an unusual project: a 13B language model trained only on pre-1930 data (260B tokens from public domain sources) to simulate a 1930s-era mind. Open weights. Enables experiments on future prediction, data contamination, and model identity.
items.content_html:
Researchers trained a 13B language model that thinks it's 1930.

It speaks like someone from a century ago, with no awareness of computers, World War II, or anything after.

The team behind it, talkie, gathered 260B tokens from public-domain books, newspapers, patents, and journals.

They built a custom filter to catch sneaky modern leaks, like footnotes added later.

For chat tuning, they skipped today's instruction datasets entirely.

Instead, they generated training pairs from old etiquette guides, letter-writing manuals, and encyclopedias.

This unlocks experiments that were impossible before:

> Test if models predict future events

> Study generalization without web contamination

> Explore how data shapes model identity

> Backtest long-term forecasting on real history

The weights are open.

A GPT-3 sized version drops this summer, with a trillion-token corpus targeting GPT-3.5 quality next.

What would a 1930 mind ask you?

The measurement “wall” you ment...
items.content:
Sorry, @peterwildeford, but this is wrong. Please don’t play along.

The measurement “wall” you mention is hit ONLY if you don’t insist on reliability.

If you demanded 95% accuracy on the task, the systems wouldn’t be close to the measurement wall. 

The measurement problem you allude to is an artifact of artificially lowered expectations.

items.title:
Wanna get a million views? Make stuff up. Take a tiny tiny bit of truth and distort it wildly.

Cons...
items.content:
Wanna get a million views? Make stuff up. Take a tiny tiny bit of truth and distort it wildly.

Consider the tweet below, 1.4M views. 

Take the chess thing. The paper that is linked doesn’t actually say that “among elite chess players people with lowest IQ are best.” or anything close to that.  It actually concludes that “When we tested the whole sample of children, some of whom had just recently started to play chess, we found a moderately positive correlation between
intelligence and chess skill” (though they found something different in a tiny, selective sample), and in any case most of the paper is about kids, not adults. [https://t.co/Gk3TK3rvaN]. 

And yeah Michael Jordan and LeBron and Kobe were shorter than Manute Bol but all were better than Muggsy Bogues and Spud Webb.  No data given for that part here.

And Robert Redford was pretty darn talented etc. No data given for acting here, either.

Wild oversimplifications will get you a million views on X.

But a lot of what you read here (and I don’t just mean this tweet) ain’t true.

It actually concludes that “When we tested the whole sample of children, some of whom had just recently started to play chess, we found a moderately positive correlation between

intelligence and chess skill” (though they found something different in a tiny, selective sample), and in any case most of the paper is about kids, not adults. [https://t.co/Gk3TK3rvaN].

And yeah Michael Jordan and LeBron and Kobe were shorter than Manute Bol but all were better than Muggsy Bogues and Spud Webb. No data given for that part here.

And Robert Redford was pretty darn talented etc. No data given for acting here, either.

Wild oversimplifications will get you a million views on X.

But a lot of what you read here (and I don’t just mean this tweet) ain’t true.

The bigger unlock is context awareness.

You can bring:
→ Multiple tabs
→ Documents
→ Images

Into o...
items.content:
The bigger unlock is context awareness.

You can bring:
→ Multiple tabs
→ Documents
→ Images

Into one query.

That means the system understands your entire research flow, not isolated searches.

This is what Chrome’s AI Mode is aiming for, and it changes how we learn and decide.

If this direction holds, “10 tabs” might feel as outdated as bookmarks.

Watch the full video.

Curious, how many tabs do you have open right now, and do you think that number goes to zero?

Don't miss out on the latest AI advancements! Sign up here to stay informed! https://t.co/MlFrT0maIM

RAG-Driven Generat...
items.content:
New Release (2nd edition) from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/3oUqMLht1G

RAG-Driven Generative AI — Build MAS-RAG with DualRAG, GraphRAG, multimodal video pipelines, and Oracle Database 23ai

𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗙𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀:
🟢Master DualRAG by combining vector search with SQL filtering over structured enterprise data.

🟢Implement GraphRAG, Spatial-RAG, and vector search natively in Oracle Database 23ai.

🟢Build multimodal video pipelines with human-feedback loops and fine-tuned models.

𝗕𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗳𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿:
🟣Bring intelligence directly to the data within Oracle Database 23ai.

🟣Defeat hallucinations and data poisoning with DualRAG, synchronizing vector semantics with structured SQL.

🟣Build MAS-RAG pipelines with Planner, Agent Registry, and MCP-standardized sovereign agents.

🟣Engineer an inference-time router using hybrid adaptive RAG to switch between reasoning, retrieval, and human feedback.

🟣Fuse vector similarity, Oracle Spatial, and SQL Property Graph traversal into a converged hyper-query.

🟣Deploy multimodal video RAG with version-controlled schema registry and semantic vector search over visual assets.

items.title:
New release from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/WyEqNc93yl

A Practical Guide to Reinforceme...
items.content:
New release from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/WyEqNc93yl

A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF).

Amazon Summary:

RLHF is a powerful approach to AI alignment and human-centered machine learning. By combining reinforcement learning algorithms with human feedback signals, RLHF has become a key method for improving the safety, reliability, and alignment of large language models (LLMs).

This book begins with the foundations of reinforcement learning and policy optimization, including algorithms such as proximal policy optimization (PPO), and explains how reward models and human preference learning help fine-tune AI systems and generative AI models.

items.title: A new learning curriculum on @ChapterPal: Prep reading for the search and retrieval systems intervie...
items.content:
A new learning curriculum on @ChapterPal: Prep reading for the search and retrieval systems interview

This curriculum provides a comprehensive foundation for understanding and building modern search and retrieval systems, bridging the gap between classical information retrieval techniques and cutting-edge neural architectures.

The scope begins with fundamental concepts such as term weighting, probabilistic models, inverted indexing, and evaluation metrics, establishing the necessary groundwork for large-scale production search engines.  Students progress to advanced topics, including the use of clickthrough data for optimization, neural ranking, and the transformative impact of deep language models like BERT and Transformer architectures.

The final modules explore sophisticated retrieval strategies—such as dense passage retrieval, late interaction models, contrastive learning, and retrieval-augmented generation—while emphasizing the practical challenges of approximate nearest neighbor search and the importance of robust zero-shot evaluation across diverse, heterogeneous datasets.

https://t.co/zUzSkTY9QG

items.title: I absolutely hate this extremely utilitarian and opportunistic view of what X is all about. Most peo...
items.content: I absolutely hate this extremely utilitarian and opportunistic view of what X is all about. Most people here are not using this app to slide into their next professional gig. X can be and should be anything you want it to be. And you shouldn’t be pigeonholed into just a single type of content. I have used X for professional content, but I also just love to fool around, post my thoughts on anything that comes to my mind, SHARE memes, connect with people, keep abreast of the latest developments (especially in AI), and overall just plain have fun.

items.title:
@ls_brd @ChapterPal There are two PDF-to-markdown options:

1. Regular OCR that calls Mistral OCR wh...
items.content:
@ls_brd @ChapterPal There are two PDF-to-markdown options:

1. Regular OCR that calls Mistral OCR which is currently the most OK OCR for the money.
2. Premium OCR which uses Gemini 3 Flash for text and home-made figure and table extractor.

So, basically, the output quality for a messy PDF will depend on how good Mistral or Gemini becomes over time.

Also, there's a reader that doesn't convert into markdown: you read the original PDF layout split into paragraphs. You can ask questions after each paragraph and then continue reading.

items.title: 🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and Data Science Competitions with Machine Learning, GenAI, ...
items.content:
🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and Data Science Competitions with Machine Learning, GenAI, and LLMs [2nd Ed.]: https://t.co/hrVstwy4zH v/ @PacktDataML

Table of Contents:
🔶Introducing Data Science Competition
🔷Organizing Data with Datasets
🔶Work & Learn with Kaggle Notebooks
🔷Kaggle Models
🔶Leveraging Discussion Forums
🔷Detailing Competition Tasks & Metrics
🔶Designing Good Validation Schemes
🔷Modeling for Tabular Competitions
🔶Hyperparameter Optimization
🔷Ensembling & Stacking Solutions
🔶Modeling Image Classification & Segmentation

My Review (on Amazon):
This 700-page masterpiece of writing covers everything you need—start to finish—to be a competitive coder, specifically for Kaggle data science competitions. The book covers the mechanics of the competitions (platform, resources, rankings, leaderboards), then the infrastructure (notebooks, GitHub, data sets, frameworks, discussion forums), and then nearly 500 pages devoted to "Elevating Your Game" (in-depth coverage of modeling techniques, evaluation metrics, validation strategies, hyperparameter optimization, ensembles, stacking, and various categories of competitions: tabular data, computer vision, NLP, Gen AI, simulations). The book concludes with a valuable section on building your Kaggle portfolio for career advancement and new opportunities. This is an outstanding data science / AI / Machine Learning training resource for anyone, even if you are not into the competitions, though especially if you are a dedicated Kaggler.

items.title:
The real problem isn’t search. It’s context switching.

When I’m researching, my flow usually looks ...
items.content:
The real problem isn’t search. It’s context switching.

When I’m researching, my flow usually looks like:
→ Search
→ Open new tab
→ Scan
→ Go back
→ Repeat

That loop kills depth.

You’re not thinking, you’re juggling.

items.title: 💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]:...
items.content:
💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]: https://t.co/Z32D8jOzxC v/ @PacktDataML

𝓒𝓸𝓷𝓽𝓮𝓷𝓽𝓼:
🔹Common Conventions & API Elements of Scikit-Learn
🔹Pre-Model Workflow and Data Preprocessing
🔹Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
🔹Build Models with Distance Metrics & Nearest Neighbors
🔹Linear Models and Regularization
🔹Advanced Logistic Regression and Extensions
🔹Support Vector Machines and Kernel Methods
🔹Tree-Based Algorithms and Ensemble Methods
🔹Text Processing and Multiclass Classification
🔹Clustering Techniques
🔹Novelty and Outlier Detection
🔹Cross-Validation and Model Evaluation Techniques
🔹Deploying Scikit-Learn Models in Production

My Review (on Amazon):
I love this book, not only for its amazing content, but also for its brilliant adherence to its "Cookbook" label. The table of contents looks repetitive, but that's the magic of it. Find the algorithm, method, or application that you want "to cook up," and the presentation follows a uniform format from chapter to chapter. This is an extraordinarily practical, useful, and reader-friendly style, perfect for the target audience: anyone who needs to use (or is discovering how to use) specific Scikit-learn tools for their Python-based machine learning tasks. Consequently, this is great not only for individual use but also for training workshops and other educational settings. Python code in a cookbook context has never looked so good. IMHO.

𝙆𝙚𝙮 𝙁𝙚𝙖𝙩𝙪𝙧𝙚𝙨:
●You work by hand, with real numbers
●You fill in the blanks instead of reading proofs
●You reason about shapes, rows, columns, and structure step by step
●You discover the math yourself, rather than being told the rules upfront

●Understand why matrix multiplication is not commutative
●Apply associativity and distributive properties to simplify work
●Use chaining and transpose concepts in real computations
●Build intuition for inverse matrices and linear equations
●Strengthen speed and accuracy through progressive practice sets
●Learn tiled multiplication ideas for efficient computation

items.title:
★ ★ ★ ★ ★ 
This 5-★ book from @PacktDataML provides a great Guide to using OpenSearch and its many a...
items.content:
★ ★ ★ ★ ★ 
This 5-★ book from @PacktDataML provides a great Guide to using OpenSearch and its many applications: https://t.co/354NCwC91R
★ ★ ★ ★ ★ 
What is OpenSearch? Google says: "OpenSearch is an open-source search and analytics suite that provides scalable full-text search, real-time application monitoring, log analytics, security analytics, and vector database capabilities. Derived from Elasticsearch as a fork, it offers a community-driven, Apache 2.0-licensed solution for data-intensive applications, along with OpenSearch Dashboards for data visualization and exploration. OpenSearch is known for its high availability, performance, and flexibility, allowing users to analyze, visualize, and build data-driven applications across various use cases."
★ ★
𝓑𝓸𝓸𝓴  𝓓𝓮𝓼𝓬𝓻𝓲𝓹𝓽𝓲𝓸𝓷:

From seasoned data professionals managing billions of records to aspiring analysts exploring diverse datasets, this guide is for users at all levels who want to make the most of OpenSearch's capabilities and functionalities. Written by distinguished AWS Solutions Architects Jon Handler, Ph.D., a former search engine developer, Prashant Agrawal, a search specialist, and Soujanya Konka, an expert in large-scale data migrations, this guide brings together deep technical expertise with practical, hands-on knowledge of implementing OpenSearch in real-world scenarios.

Starting with an introduction to OpenSearch, you’ll get to grips with the key features before delving into essential topics such as installing OpenSearch, ingesting data, crafting queries, visualizing results, ensuring security, and optimizing performance. Each concept is accompanied by practical examples and tutorials, allowing you to grasp the material through hands-on experience.

Derived from Elasticsearch as a fork, it offers a community-driven, Apache 2.0-licensed solution for data-intensive applications, along with OpenSearch Dashboards for data visualization and exploration. OpenSearch is known for its high availability, performance, and flexibility, allowing users to analyze, visualize, and build data-driven applications across various use cases."

★ ★

𝓑𝓸𝓸𝓴 𝓓𝓮𝓼𝓬𝓻𝓲𝓹𝓽𝓲𝓸𝓷:

From seasoned data professionals managing billions of records to aspiring analysts exploring diverse datasets, this guide is for users at all levels who want to make the most of OpenSearch's capabilities and functionalities. Written by distinguished AWS Solutions Architects Jon Handler, Ph.D., a former search engine developer, Prashant Agrawal, a search specialist, and Soujanya Konka, an expert in large-scale data migrations, this guide brings together deep technical expertise with practical, hands-on knowledge of implementing OpenSearch in real-world scenarios.

Starting with an introduction to OpenSearch, you’ll get to grips with the key features before delving into essential topics such as installing OpenSearch, ingesting data, crafting queries, visualizing results, ensuring security, and optimizing performance. Each concept is accompanied by practical examples and tutorials, allowing you to grasp the material through hands-on experience.

items.title: @scaling01 it’s funny, i actually agree with most the first part. on the second, you and many other ...
items.content: @scaling01 it’s funny, i actually agree with most the first part. on the second, you and many other people misremember (or never read) what I said in “deep learning is hitting a wall”, which was neither about revenue or AI’s upper limits. Rather, it was an argument that purely scaling LLMs would not get us to AGI, and that we would need to start including symbolic tools and working towards neurosymbolic AI.  I was quite right about that, and that’s what happening now.

items.title: @gonlenidefi @ChapterPal There is an option to generate an executive summary of the entire paper/cha...

items.summary: Burkov discusses ChapterPal features for paper summarization and reading.
items.reasoning: Product discussion with minimal engagement.
items.summary_html: Burkov discusses ChapterPal features for paper summarization and reading.
items.content_html:
@gonlenidefi @ChapterPal There is an option to generate an executive summary of the entire paper/chapter or of each section. But the goal here is to let you decide whether you already know this and should skip it, or if you should read it.

For synthesis of multiple papers, I guess NotebookLM does fine.

I'm thinking about making ChapterPal's curricula talkable to, but I'm not sure if it's too much of a deviation from the reader concept.

items.title: Cracking the Cosmic Code: The Man Who’s Been Debugging the Universe for Decades to Crack the Source ...
items.content:
Cracking the Cosmic Code: The Man Who’s Been Debugging the Universe for Decades to Crack the Source Code 

Michael has been debugging the universe from decades at least along with other scientists for 21 years. Could the Second Law of Infodynamics deliver the ‘smoking gun’ evidence? This intriguing concept suggests a fundamental principle governing the flow of information in the universe, much like the Second Law of Thermodynamics that governs the energy. If Infodynamics concept has been substantiated, the theory could provide profound insights into the nature of entropy and the formation of complex systems and spatial structures including the beginning of the big bang. Researchers have long sought definitive evidence that links information processes to the physical laws that shape our universe. If the Second Law of Infodynamics holds true, it could offer a pivotal piece of the puzzle in understanding how life and complexity emerge, potentially revolutionizing our grasp of physics and information theory.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode  

For over 21 years, a number of scientists have speculated about the intriguing idea that our existence might be an incredibly intricate simulation. Some argue that the search for "glitches" can reveal evidence of a simulation. However, Michael from the University of Portsmouth presents a different perspective. He suggests that the universe's tendency toward symmetry might be interpreted as a form of a compression algorithm in line with his proposed "Second Law of Thermodynamics." 

In 2003, philosopher Nick Bostrom from the University of Oxford introduced the provocative idea that our perceived reality might actually be a hyper advanced simulation crafted by beings with immense technological abilities. Since Bostrom's influential hypothesis, scientists have been contemplating ways to uncover evidence that could support this theory and exploring the possibility of escaping the simulation entirely.

Philosopher Francheshi mentioned to Gizmodo in December that the idea of living in a simulation could be tested in reality.  Michael Vospon believes that there is some kind of flaw in the Universe's source code that needs to be observed and discovered, such as a remote area of the universe that remains unclear to telescopes. However, he also noted that an even more advanced simulation could potentially roll back space time continuum, identify the flaws, and restart the entire simulation.

Michael, a physicist at the University of Portsmouth in the U.K., suggests that while searching for a flaw or glitch in the simulation might offer credible evidence, investigating a sort of "source code" of the universe could present a more compelling avenue for demonstrating our artificial existence.

The concept known as the Second Law of Infodynamics posits that information entropy “must remain constant or decrease over time—up to a minimum value at equilibrium,” as articulated by Michael in a 2023 article for The Conversation. InfoDynamics has alredy been applied to several fields of science on earth and he further argues that this principle extends to the behavior of genetic information, asserting that it is not merely random, as proposed by Charles Darwin, but it goes against the theory of evoluation and instead consistently strives to minimize information entropy. The theory that is proposed by Michael's compelling assertion that the universe inherently favors symmetry over asymmetry, operating as an optimization program or as the “most effective data compression” mechanism imaginable.   Michael's perspective to view universe as a simulation created from another dimension with source code and program and the runtime binding as data and space time continuum not only deepens our understanding of information theory but also challenges us to reconsider the fundamental principles that govern our reality, illuminating the intricate relationship between information and the cosmos itself.

References 

Orf, D. (2025, March 27). A scientist thinks we live in a simulation—and that he’s found proof of the universe’s source code. Popular Mechanics. Retrieved March 31, 2025, from https://t.co/dykAy5mO7p 

Vopson, M. M. (2023, October 6). The second law of infodynamics and its implications for the simulated universe hypothesis. AIP Advances, 13(10), 105308. https://t.co/CgzgEydbQk. Retrieved March 31, 2025.
Correspondence: melvin.vopson@port.ac.uk

items.title:
The Framework 13 Pro I'm using is running the Intel Core Ultra 5 325, and I'm yet to hear the fans.
...
items.content:
The Framework 13 Pro I'm using is running the Intel Core Ultra 5 325, and I'm yet to hear the fans.

I haven't done anything super crazy with it yet, but I've played videos, ran a Docker container with a large project, and have done normal things I do everyday, and nothing yet.

I'll keep trying, but so far, the laptop is 100% silent, hasn't gotten hot, and I'm yet to hear the fans.

I'm looking forward to my Ultra X9 388H. I imagine that one will be even harder to bother.

items.title:
Subsidies in Europe are generally a net negative because they change incentives

People won't compet...
items.content:
Subsidies in Europe are generally a net negative because they change incentives

People won't compete to become the best at their job, they'll just compete who can win the most subsidies

So you get below average people doing below average work but getting paid for it

items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: LeCun discussing the historical misuse of 'socialist' in party names, citing Nazi party as example
items.reasoning: Political/historical discussion unrelated to AI
items.summary_html: LeCun discussing the historical misuse of 'socialist' in party names, citing Nazi party as example
items.content_html:
@Tripnips22 @MetsYeet @elonmusk From Gemini:

The National Socialist German Workers' Party (NSDAP):

Commonly known as the Nazi Party, this is the most famous example. While the name included "Socialist" and "Workers," the party’s ideology was diametrically opposed to traditional socialist concepts like class struggle and the abolition of private property. Instead, they promoted Volksgemeinschaft (a racial national community). Once in power, the Nazis suppressed trade unions and oversaw large-scale privatization.

items.title: @MetsYeet @elonmusk And like saying that the Deutsche Demokratische Republik was ever Demokratische....
items.content:
@MetsYeet @elonmusk And like saying that the Deutsche Demokratische Republik was ever Demokratische. 

And like saying that the Democratic People's Republic of Korea was ever democratic or about by the people. 

And like saying that the People's Republic of China was ever run by the people.

Congo, Laos, Algeria, and Ethiopia all have authoritarian governments yet have "democratic" in their name.

Historically, many far-right parties have used the word "socialist" in their name to swindle supporters into thinking they care about them.

@marclou @edibudimilic Oh for sure, she'll love you fine in 20m2 but that still means she's going to want 200m2

85% of all consumer purchasing is decided by women

If women didn't exist, I don't think we'd buy houses at all, it's all for them to have a nest and grow kids in, which is fine

It's also nice for us but not our primal goal which is why it feels weird and ideal to be in 20m2, that's like a military barack where you station to fight a war, which is what mean do biologically
